subject: General technical documentation authoring: document type selection, audience fit, progressive disclosure, and docs-as-code discipline

evals.prompt: A project needs to document a new internal API. The author is unsure whether to write a reference doc, a tutorial, or an architecture note. What does the documentation skill say about choosing the right document type?
Why does the documentation skill treat audience fit as a required verification item rather than a nice-to-have?
A document about UI accessibility behavior arrives for review. Should the reviewer use the documentation skill or the a11y skill? Cite the negative-routing rule that decides this.

A reviewer opens an API reference doc that spends the first two screens walking the reader through a multi-step tutorial before the actual field definitions appear. Which anti-pattern from the documentation skill does this match, and what is the prescribed fix?

A pull request arrives with a failing integration test. The author asks whether they should invoke the documentation skill to write a new runbook page explaining the test, or use a different skill. Which skill handles failure diagnosis, and cite the boundary rule that decides this.

An engineer is extracting a duplicated helper function across three files and wants documentation input on how to restructure the code. Should the documentation skill activate, and if not, which skill does? Cite the negative-routing rule.

A reviewer opens a new architecture note and finds that the central design decision (why Postgres was chosen over DynamoDB) appears in paragraph nine, after eight paragraphs of context. Which verification rule does this violate, and how should the author restructure the doc?
